text.title: ADR Supersession Detection Core Rules
text.summary: Four testable rules every application of ADR Supersession Detection enforces. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: Every ADR Supersession Detection detector has a documented detector ID, signal definition, threshold, and remediation runbook URL. Detector without remediation is alarm fatigue.

rationale.source: alert design / Mike Julian, Practical Monitoring (2017)

rule.rationale: Alarm-only detectors train operators to ignore alerts. Linked remediation lifts response from ad-hoc to routine.

rule.statement: False-positive rate target is set before deploy AND measured monthly. Detectors above target are tuned or retired.

rationale.source: alerting literature / Google SRE, Practical Alerting

rule.rationale: FP-rate creep degrades trust. Periodic review forces the choice: tune or remove.

rule.statement: Detector firings include enough context (last 5 log lines, run ID, owner) to start triage WITHOUT a second tool query.

rationale.source: observability practice / Charity Majors, Observability Engineering (2022)

rule.rationale: Alerts that demand 3-5 follow-up queries before triage starts add minutes per page.

rule.statement: Detectors are tested with synthetic + replayed real failures BEFORE production rollout. Untested detectors fire wrong in unexpected modes.

rationale.source: chaos engineering / Basiri et al., Chaos Engineering at Netflix

rule.rationale: Untested detectors fail closed (silent) or open (storm). Both worse than no detector.

example.title: Detector with remediation link
example.code: Detector: D-CSRF-01 — request lacks anti-CSRF token header Threshold: any request to /admin without X-CSRF-Token Remediation: docs/runbooks/csrf-fix.md
example.note: Trigger and remediation are bound. Operators can act without context-switching.

example.title: Alarm-only detector
example.note: No specifics, no remediation. Alarm fatigue.
